title: Simulate the number of relatives under different fertility and sibling models
text:
We simulate the number of relatives for each individual using fertility and sibling distributions.




Code
# The fertility distribution modeled as a zero-inflated negative binomial distribution.
simulate_relatives_zinb <- function(mus, sizes, pis,
                                    n_sim = 10000,
                                    max_kids = 12) {
  draw_fert <- function(n, mu, size, pi) {
    ifelse(runif(n) < pi, 0L,
           as.integer(pmin(rnbinom(n, size, mu = mu), max_kids)))
  }
  
  draw_sibs <- function(n, mu, size) {
    as.integer(pmin(rnbinom(n, size + 1, mu = mu * (size + 1) / size), max_kids - 1))
  }
  # 1. Parents and grandparents are fixed at 2 and 4, respectively.
  parents <- rep(2L, n_sim)
  grandparents <- rep(4L, n_sim)

  # 2. Siblings for the focal individual
  siblings <- draw_sibs(n = n_sim, mu = mus[2], size = sizes[2])
  
  # 3. Children of the focal individual
  children <- draw_fert(n = n_sim, mu = mus[1], size = sizes[1], pi = pis[1])

  # 4. Aunts & uncles (no subtraction needed)
  aunts_uncles <- purrr::map_int(parents, function(n_par) {
    as.integer(sum(draw_sibs(n_par, mu = mus[3], size = sizes[3])))
  })

  # 5. Nieces & nephews: for each sibling, simulate their fertility and sum
  nieces_nephews <- purrr::map_int(siblings, function(n_sib) {
    if (n_sib == 0L) 0L else as.integer(sum(draw_fert(n_sib, mu = mus[1], size = sizes[1], pi = pis[1])))
  })

  # 6. Cousins: for each aunt/uncle, simulate their fertility and sum
  cousins <- purrr::map_int(aunts_uncles, function(n_au) {
    if (n_au == 0L) 0L else as.integer(sum(draw_fert(n_au, mu = mus[2], size = sizes[2], pi = pis[2])))
  })

  tibble::tibble(
    Parents    = parents,
    Grandparents = grandparents,
    Siblings    = siblings,
    Children    = children,
    AuntUncles = aunts_uncles,
    NieceNephews = nieces_nephews,
    Cousins     = cousins
  )
}

# The fertility distribution modeled as a Poisson distribution.
simulate_relatives_poisson <- function(mus, 
                                       n_sim = 10000,
                                       max_kids = 12) {
  draw_fert <- function(n, mu) {
    as.integer(pmin(rpois(n, lambda = mu), max_kids))
  }
  
  draw_sibs <- function(n, mu, size) {
    as.integer(pmin(rpois(n, lambda = mu), max_kids - 1))
  }
  # 1. Parents and grandparents are fixed at 2 and 4, respectively.
  parents <- rep(2L, n_sim)
  grandparents <- rep(4L, n_sim)

  # 2. Siblings for the focal individual
  siblings <- draw_sibs(n = n_sim, mu = mus[2])
  
  # 3. Children of the focal individual
  children <- draw_fert(n = n_sim, mu = mus[1])

  # 4. Aunts & uncles (no subtraction needed)
  aunts_uncles <- purrr::map_int(parents, function(n_par) {
    as.integer(sum(draw_sibs(n_par, mu = mus[3])))
  })

  # 5. Nieces & nephews: for each sibling, simulate their fertility and sum
  nieces_nephews <- purrr::map_int(siblings, function(n_sib) {
    if (n_sib == 0L) 0L else as.integer(sum(draw_fert(n_sib, mu = mus[1])))
  })

  # 6. Cousins: for each aunt/uncle, simulate their fertility and sum
  cousins <- purrr::map_int(aunts_uncles, function(n_au) {
    if (n_au == 0L) 0L else as.integer(sum(draw_fert(n_au, mu = mus[2])))
  })

  tibble::tibble(
    Parents    = parents,
    Grandparents = grandparents,
    Siblings    = siblings,
    Children    = children,
    AuntUncles = aunts_uncles,
    NieceNephews = nieces_nephews,
    Cousins     = cousins
  )
}

# The fertility distribution fixed at the mean size.
simulate_relatives_fixed <- function(ferts, sibs) {
  # 1. Parents and grandparents are fixed at 2 and 4, respectively.
  parents <- 2
  grandparents <- 4

  # 2. Siblings for the focal individual
  siblings <- sibs[2]
  
  # 3. Children of the focal individual
  children <- ferts[1]

  # 4. Aunts & uncles (no subtraction needed)
  aunts_uncles <- parents * sibs[3]

  # 5. Nieces & nephews: for each sibling, simulate their fertility and sum
  nieces_nephews <- siblings * ferts[1]

  # 6. Cousins: for each aunt/uncle, simulate their fertility and sum
  cousins <- aunts_uncles * ferts[2]

  tibble::tibble(
    Parents    = parents,
    Grandparents = grandparents,
    Siblings    = siblings,
    Children    = children,
    AuntUncles = aunts_uncles,
    NieceNephews = nieces_nephews,
    Cousins     = cousins
  )
}

# Read the fitted parameters and empirical summaries written by fert_model.qmd
# instead of hard-coding them, so this notebook stays in sync with the data.
fert_params <- read_csv(file.path(output_path, "fertility_parameters.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)
fert_estimation <- read_csv(file.path(output_path, "fertility_estimation.csv"), show_col_types = FALSE)

# Generations used below, in order: focal individual, parent, grandparent.
gen_years <- c(1990, 1970, 1950)

zinb_params <- fert_params %>% filter(Model == "ZINB")
pois_params <- fert_params %>% filter(Model == "Poisson")
sib_estimation <- fert_estimation %>% filter(distribution == "sibling")

mus_zinb <- zinb_params$mu[match(gen_years, zinb_params$Year)]
sizes_zinb <- zinb_params$size[match(gen_years, zinb_params$Year)]
pis_zinb <- zinb_params$pi0[match(gen_years, zinb_params$Year)]
mus_poisson <- pois_params$mu[match(gen_years, pois_params$Year)]
mean_ferts <- mus_poisson
mean_sibs <- sib_estimation$mean_data[match(gen_years, sib_estimation$Year)]

n_sim <- 100000







Code
relative_counts = list()
relative_counts[[1]] = simulate_relatives_fixed(
  ferts = mean_ferts,
  sibs = mean_sibs
)
relative_counts[[2]] = simulate_relatives_poisson(
  mus = mus_poisson,
  n_sim = n_sim
)
relative_counts[[3]] = simulate_relatives_zinb(
  mus = mus_zinb,
  sizes = sizes_zinb,
  pis = pis_zinb,
  n_sim = n_sim
)







Code
relative_model_names <- c("fixed", "poisson", "zinb")

relative_summary <- Map(function(dat, model_name) {
  tibble(
    Relative = names(dat),
    mean = as.numeric(colMeans(dat)),
    variance = if (model_name == "fixed") {
      rep(0, ncol(dat))
    } else {
      as.numeric(sapply(dat, var))
    },
    model = model_name
  )
}, relative_counts, relative_model_names) %>%
  bind_rows()

relative_summary_table <- relative_summary %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = model,
    values_from = c(mean, variance),
    names_glue = "{.value}_{model}"
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(
    Relative,
    mean_fixed,
    mean_poisson,
    mean_zinb,
    variance_fixed,
    variance_poisson,
    variance_zinb
  )

write.csv(relative_summary_table, file.path(output_path, "relative_summary_table.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

kable(
  relative_summary_table,
  digits = 3,
  caption = "Mean and variance of relative counts by fertility and sibling model."
)

relative_distribution <- bind_rows(
  relative_counts[[2]] %>% mutate(Model = "Poisson"),
  relative_counts[[3]] %>% mutate(Model = "ZINB")
) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = -Model,
    names_to = "Relative",
    values_to = "Count"
  ) %>%
  filter(!Relative %in% c("Parents", "Grandparents")) %>%
  mutate(
    Relative = recode(
      Relative,
      "Siblings" = "Siblings",
      "Children" = "Children",
      "AuntUncles" = "Aunts and uncles",
      "NieceNephews" = "Nieces and nephews",
      "Cousins" = "First cousins"
    ),
    Relative = factor(
      Relative,
      levels = c(
        "Siblings", "Children", "Aunts and uncles",
        "Nieces and nephews", "First cousins"
      )
    )
  )

ggplot(relative_distribution, aes(x = Model, y = Count, fill = Model)) +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.65, alpha = 0.85, outlier.size = 0.35) +
  facet_wrap(~ Relative, scales = "free_y", nrow = 1) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Poisson" = "#0072B2", "ZINB" = "#D55E00")) +
  labs(
    # title = "Relative Count Distributions Under Poisson and ZINB Models",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Relative count",
    fill = "Model"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank()
  )

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
text: In this article, we develop a statistical framework for modelling the relationship between the fertility distribution and sibling distribution: Given a fertility distribution, we derive what the resulting sibling distribution would be in the next generation. Specifically, the fertility distribution describes the number of children per woman, while the sibling distribution describes the number of siblings per person in the next generation.

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
text:
1.1 Poisson distribution
The Poisson distribution is a popular model for count data, particularly when the mean and variance are equal. However, it may not be very suitable for fertility data, which often exhibit zero-inflation and overdispersion (variance greater than the mean). The Poisson distribution with mean fertility (\(\mu\)) is defined as: for the number of children \(k \geq 0\), \[P(N_{child}=k)=e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^k}{k!}, \tag{1}\] denoted by \(\text{Poisson}(\mu)\). The mean and variance of \(\text{Poisson}(\mu)\) are both \(\mu\).

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
text:
1.2 Zero inflated negative binomial distribution
The zero inflated negative binomial distribution with parameters mean fertility (\(\mu\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta\)), and zero‑inflation probability (\(\pi_0\))—denoted as \(\text{ZINB}(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\)—is defined as: for the number of children \(k \geq 0\), \[P(N_{child}=k)=\begin{cases}
\pi_0 + (1-\pi_0)p^{\theta} & \text{if } k=0, \\
(1-\pi_0)\binom{k+\theta-1}{k}(1-p)^k p ^{\theta} & \text{if } k \geq 1,
\end{cases} \tag{2}\] where \(p = \frac{\theta}{\mu+\theta}\) is denoted as the success probability. For the negative binomial distribution \(\text{NB}(\mu,\theta)\), the mean and variance are \(\mu\) and \(\mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}\). Therefore, the mean and variance of \(\text{ZINB}(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\) are given by \(\mu(1-\pi_0)\) and \(\mu(1-\pi_0) + \frac{\mu^2(1-\pi_0)}{\theta}+\mu^2\pi_0(1-\pi_0)\), respectively.

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
text:
2.1 Sibship size paradox
A common mistake is that the sibling distribution should be the same as the fertility distribution minus one. However, this is not the case due to the sibship size paradox: individuals with more siblings are more likely to be sampled in the population, leading to a bias in the observed sibling distribution compared to the underlying fertility distribution.
For example, if we have a population of \(N_0\) women where \(50\%\) of women have 0 children, \(25\%\) of women have 1 child, and \(25\%\) of women have 2 children, the fertility distribution is \(P(N_{child}=0)=0.5\), \(P(N_{child}=1)=0.25\), and \(P(N_{child}=2)=0.25\). Thus, in the next generation, the population size is \[N_1 = 0.5N_0 \cdot 0 + 0.25N_0 \cdot 1 + 0.25N_0 \cdot 2 = 0.75N_0.\] And \(0.25N_0 \cdot 1\) individuals have 0 siblings, \(0.25N_0 \cdot 2\) individuals have 1 sibling. Therefore, the sibling distribution is \[P(N_{sib}=0)=\frac{0.25N_0}{0.75N_0}=\frac{1}{3},\quad P(N_{sib}=1)=\frac{0.25N_0 \cdot 2}{0.75N_0}=\frac{2}{3}.\]
To conclude, most people come from large families even when most families are small. This apparent paradox occurs because individuals with more siblings are sampled with an importance weight proportional to their family size.
Formally, the relationship between the fertility distribution and sibling distribution can be expressed as follows: Let \(N_{child}\) be the random variable representing the number of children per woman, and \(N_{sib}\) be the random variable representing the number of siblings per person in the next generation. Then, for any count \(k \geq 0\), the probability that an individual has \(k\) siblings is proportional to the probability that their mother has \(k+1\) children, multiplied by the number of ways the individual could be one of those children. Therefore, we have: \[P(N_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)P(N_{child}=k+1). \tag{3}\] This relationship holds for any fertility distribution, and the sibling distribution can be derived accordingly.

title: Relationship between Fertility Distribution and Sibling Distribution
section: 2.2 Sibling distribution for different fertility distributions

title: About
text: This project studies how fertility distributions shape sibling and broader kin-count distributions. Using statistical models of fertility and family structure, we estimate empirical fertility patterns, derive sibling distributions, and simulate relative counts across relationship categories.

title: About
text: This project studies how fertility distributions shape sibling and broader kin-count distributions. Using statistical models of fertility and family structure, we estimate empirical fertility patterns, derive sibling distributions, and simulate relative counts across relationship categories.

title: About
text:
2 Methods

2.1 Fertility Modeling
We use a zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) distribution to model fertility patterns:
\[P(X = k) = \begin{cases}
\pi + (1 - \pi) \cdot NB(0; \mu, \theta) & \text{if } k = 0 \\
(1 - \pi) \cdot NB(k; \mu, \theta) & \text{if } k > 0
\end{cases}\]
where: - \(\pi\) is the zero-inflation probability (excess childlessness) - \(\mu\) is the mean of the negative binomial component - \(\theta\) is the dispersion parameter


2.2 Sibling Distribution
The sibling distribution is derived from the fertility distribution, using the relationship: \[P(num_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)P(num_{child}=k+1).\]

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
We extract the fertility information for U.S. women from the Integrated Public Use Microdata Series (IPUMS) dataset.




Code
processed_path <- if (dir.exists("data")) {
  file.path("data", "processed")
} else {
  file.path("..", "data", "processed")
}
output_path <- if (dir.exists("output")) "output" else file.path("..", "output")

fertility_counts <- read_csv(
  file.path(processed_path, "ipums_fertility_counts_by_cohort.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

fertility_data <- fertility_counts %>%
  tidyr::uncount(n_women) %>%
  dplyr::select(Year, cohort, n_child)


The processed fertility counts contain:

Year – cohort reference year (1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990)
cohort – birth cohort label
n_child – children ever born, with 12 representing the 12+ top-coded category
n_women – number of women in that cohort/count cell




We focus on U.S. women aged 50-59, who are considered to have completed their fertility. The Census data include observations from 1960, 1970, 1980, and 1990. Women who were aged 50-59 in 1950 are observed as ages 60-69 in the 1960 Census. We therefore prepare fertility data for U.S. women aged 50-59 in 1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, and 1990, corresponding to the 1891-1900, 1901-1910, 1911-1920, 1921-1930, and 1931-1940 birth cohorts, respectively.

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
2.1 Fertility distribution
We model the number of children per family using a Poisson distribution and a zero inflated negative binomial distribution (ZINB), respectively.

2.1.1 Poisson distribution
The Poisson distribution is a common model for count data, particularly when the mean and variance are equal. However, it may not be suitable for fertility data, which often exhibit zero-inflation and overdispersion (variance greater than the mean). The Poisson distribution with mean fertility (\(\mu\)) is defined as: for a count \(k \geq 0\), \[P(num_{child}=k)=e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^k}{k!},\] denoted by \(Poisson(\mu)\). The mean and variance of \(Poisson(\mu)\) are both \(\mu\).


2.1.2 Zero inflated negative binomial distribution
The zero inflated negative binomial distribution with parameters mean fertility (\(\mu\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta\)), and zero‑inflation probability (\(\pi_0\))—denoted as \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\)—is defined as: for a count \(k \geq 0\), \[P(num_{child}=k)=\begin{cases}
\pi_0 + (1-\pi_0)p^{\theta} & \text{if } k=0, \\
(1-\pi_0)\binom{k+\theta-1}{k}(1-p)^k p ^{\theta} & \text{if } k \geq 1,
\end{cases}\] where \(p = \frac{\theta}{\mu+\theta}\) represents the success probability of the negative binomial distribution.
According to the definition of the negative binomial distribution, the mean and variance of \(NB(\mu,\theta)\) are given by \(\mu\) and \(\mu + \frac{\mu^2}{\theta}\), respectively. Therefore, the mean and variance of \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\) are defined by \(\mu(1-\pi_0)\) and \(\mu(1-\pi_0) + \frac{\mu^2(1-\pi_0)}{\theta}+\mu^2\pi_0(1-\pi_0)\), respectively.

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
2.2 Sibling distribution
The sibling distribution can be derived from the fertility distribution. For any count \(k \geq 0\), the probability that an individual has \(k\) siblings is proportional to the probability that their mother has \(k+1\) children, multiplied by the number of ways the individual could be one of those children. Therefore, the sibling distribution satisfies: \[P(num_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)P(num_{child}=k+1).\]

2.2.1 Derivation of sibling distribution
If the fertility distribution is \(Poisson(\mu)\), then the sibling distribution is given by: for a count \(k \geq 0\), \[P(num_{sib}=k) \propto (k+1)e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^{k+1}}{(k+1)!} = e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^{k+1}}{k!}\propto e^{-\mu}\frac{\mu^k}{k!},\] which is also a Poisson distribution with mean \(\mu\).
If the fertility distribution is \(ZINB(\mu, \theta, \pi_0)\), then the sibling distribution is given by: for a count \(k \geq 0\), \[\begin{align*}
P(num_{sib}=k) & \propto (1-\pi_0)(k+1)\binom{k+\theta}{k+1}(1-p)^{k+1} p^{\theta} \\
& = (1-\pi_0)(k+1)\frac{(k+\theta)!}{(k+1)!(\theta-1)!}(1-p)^{k+1} p^{\theta} \\
& \propto \frac{\theta(1-p)}{p} \frac{(k+\theta)!}{k!\theta!}(1-p)^k p^{\theta+1} \\
& \propto \binom{k+\theta}{k}(1-p)^k p^{\theta+1}, \\
\end{align*}\] where \(p = \frac{\theta}{\mu+\theta}\). Therefore, the sibling distribution is a negative binomial distribution with parameters mean fertility (\(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}\)), dispersion/size (\(\theta+1\)), denoted as \(NB(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta}, \theta+1)\). The variance is \(\frac{\mu(\theta+1)}{\theta} + \frac{\mu^2(\theta+1)}{\theta^2}\).

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
section: 3.1 Fit the Poisson and ZINB models
text:
3.1 Fit the Poisson and ZINB models


Code
fit_fert <- function(dat) {
  ## Poisson model
  pois <- glm(n_child ~ 1, family = poisson, data = dat)
  
  ## Zero‑inflated negative binomial model
  zinb <- zeroinfl(n_child ~ 1 | 1, data = dat, dist = "negbin")
  
  tibble(
    Model = c("Poisson", "ZINB"),
    mu    = c(exp(coef(pois)[1]),                    ## log‑link → exp() gives mean
              exp(zinb$coefficients$count[1])),
    size  = c(Inf,                                   ## dispersion θ; Inf = Poisson
              zinb$theta),
    pi0   = c(0,                                      ## zero‑inflation prob.
              plogis(zinb$coefficients$zero[1]))
  )
}

param_fert <- fertility_data %>%
  group_by(Year) %>%
  group_modify(~ fit_fert(.x)) %>%
  ungroup()

# Write the fitted parameters so downstream notebooks read them instead of
# hard-coding the values.
write.csv(param_fert, file.path(output_path, "fertility_parameters.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

section: 3.3 Compare the mean and variance from the models and data
text:
3.3 Compare the mean and variance from the models and data


Code
cal_mean_var <- function(model, mu, size, pi0) {
  if (model == "Poisson") {
    mean_fert = mu
    var_fert  = mu
    mean_sib = mu
    var_sib  = mu
  } else if (model == "ZINB") {
    mean_fert = mu * (1 - pi0)
    var_fert  = mu * (1 - pi0) + (mu^2 * (1 - pi0) / size) + (mu^2 * pi0 * (1 - pi0))
    mean_sib = mu * ((size + 1) / size)
    var_sib  = mu * ((size + 1) / size) + (mu^2 * ((size + 1) / size) / size)
  }
  
  tibble(
    Model = model,
    Mean_Fertility = mean_fert,
    Variance_Fertility = var_fert,
    Mean_Sibling = mean_sib,
    Variance_Sibling = var_sib
  )
}

mean_var_fitted <- param_fert %>%
  rowwise(Year, Model) %>%
  reframe(cal_mean_var(Model, mu, size, pi0)) %>%
  ungroup()

mean_var_data <- bind_rows(
  fertility_data %>%
    group_by(Year) %>%
    summarise(
      distribution = "fertility",
      mean_data = mean(n_child),
      variance_data = var(n_child),
      .groups = "drop"
    ),
  sib_data %>%
    group_by(Year) %>%
    summarise(
      distribution = "sibling",
      mean_data = mean(n_sib),
      variance_data = var(n_sib),
      .groups = "drop"
    )
)

mean_var_model <- mean_var_fitted %>%
  transmute(
    Year,
    Model = tolower(Model),
    mean_fertility = Mean_Fertility,
    variance_fertility = Variance_Fertility,
    mean_sibling = Mean_Sibling,
    variance_sibling = Variance_Sibling
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(mean_fertility, variance_fertility, mean_sibling, variance_sibling),
    names_to = c(".value", "distribution"),
    names_pattern = "(mean|variance)_(fertility|sibling)"
  ) %>%
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = Model,
    values_from = c(mean, variance),
    names_glue = "{.value}_{Model}"
  )

mean_var <- mean_var_data %>%

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
3.4 Model evaluation
Based on the table of mean and variance, we can see that the fertility data exhibits overdispersion (variance greater than mean). The Poisson Model assumes equal mean and variance, which does not fit the data well. The ZINB Model accounts for overdispersion and zero-inflation, providing a better fit to the data. The ZINB model’s mean and variance estimates for both fertility and sibling distributions align more closely with the observed data.

section: 4.2 Compare sibling counts from simulations with theoretical values
text:
4.2 Compare sibling counts from simulations with theoretical values
We run simulations for fertility means of 1.5, 2.0, 2.5, 3.0, 3.5, and 4.0 for both Poisson and ZINB models. Additionally, in ZINB models, we set the dispersion parameter \(\theta\) to 4, and the zero-inflation probability \(\pi_0\) to 0.05.
We compare the empirical mean and variance of sibling counts from the simulated data with the theoretical values. The comparison shows that the theoretical values align closely with the simulated results, confirming the correctness of our derivation.

size = 4
pi0 = 0.05

cal_mean_var_simulated <- function(sim_data) {
  sib_counts <- sim_data$sib_counts
  
  mean_sib = mean(sib_counts)
  var_sib = var(sib_counts)
  
  tibble(
    Mean_Sibling = mean_sib,
    Variance_Sibling = var_sib
  )
}

mean_var_zinb <- lapply(means, function(mu) cal_mean_var("ZINB", mu/(1-pi0), size, pi0)) %>% bind_rows()
threo_zinb_results <- tibble(
  Fertility_Parameter = sapply(means, function(mu) paste0("Fertility Mean = ", mu)),
  Value_Type = "Theoretical ZINB",
  Mean_Sibling = mean_var_zinb$Mean_Sibling,
  Variance_Sibling = mean_var_zinb$Variance_Sibling
)

zinb_results <- bind_rows(simulated_zinb_results, threo_zinb_results) %>%
  arrange(Fertility_Parameter) %>%
  mutate(Value_Type = factor(Value_Type, levels = c("Theoretical ZINB", "Simulated ZINB")))

kable(zinb_results, digits = 3, caption = "Mean and Variance of Sibling counts from simulations and theoretical values based on ZINB distribution.")

title: Fertility Modelling and Sibling Estimates
text:
4.3 Visualize sibling distributions
We generate discrete probability distributions for sibling counts based on both Poisson and ZINB models, with the y-axis representing the number of siblings and the x-axis representing the probability of having that many siblings. The fertility mean is set to 2.5, and the ZINB model has a dispersion parameter of 4 and a zero-inflation probability of 0.05.


Code
library(viridisLite)

plot_pmf <- function(pmf_df) {
  cats = sort(unique(pmf_df$sib_counts))
  n_cat <- length(cats)
  color_palette <- viridis(n_cat, option = "D")
  df <- pmf_df %>%
    mutate(y = ifelse(Model == "Poisson", -prob, prob),
           count_cat = factor(sib_counts, levels = cats))
  ggplot(df, aes(x = count_cat, y = y, fill = count_cat, alpha = Model)) +
    geom_col() +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "black", size = 0.5) +
    coord_flip() +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) abs(x)) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = color_palette, name = "Siblings Count") +
    scale_alpha_manual(values = c("Poisson" = 0.7, "ZINB" = 1), guide = "none") +
    labs(
      title = "PMF of Siblings Counts (Simulated from Poisson and ZINB Models)",
      x = "Number of Siblings",
      y = "Probability",
      caption = "Poisson (left, negative y), ZINB (right, positive y)."
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(size = 14, hjust = 0.5),
      axis.text.y = element_text(size = 10),
      plot.caption = element_text(face = "bold", size = 10),
      legend.position = "right",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.minor.y = element_blank()
    )
}




Code
# fertility parameters
mean = 2.5
size = 4
pi0 = 0.05
max_sibs = 12

# simulated data for fertility mean 2.5
simulated_data <- bind_rows(
  tibble(sib_counts = simulate_fert_sib("Poisson", mean, Inf, 0)$sib_counts) %>%
    mutate(Model = "Poisson"),
  tibble(sib_counts = simulate_fert_sib("ZINB", mean/(1-pi0), size, pi0)$sib_counts) %>%
    mutate(Model = "ZINB")
)

simulated_data$sib_counts <- ifelse(simulated_data$sib_counts > max_sibs, max_sibs, simulated_data$sib_counts)

pmf_data <- simulated_data %>%
  count(Model, sib_counts, name = "n_counts") %>%
  group_by(Model) %>%
  mutate(prob = n_counts / sum(n_counts)) %>%
  ungroup()

# write the pmf data to a csv file
write.csv(pmf_data, file = file.path(output_path, "pmf_sibling_counts.csv"), row.names = FALSE)

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
text:
This notebook converts a restricted IPUMS USA extract into the aggregate fertility summaries used throughout the kincounts project, and documents the processing decisions. It makes two analytic contributions:
Because IPUMS USA policy restricts redistribution of raw microdata, only the aggregate CSVs in data/processed/ are committed; the raw extract stays local-only (ignored by Git via .gitignore). Processing code is collapsed by default; the sections below present the resulting figures and tables.

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
text:
1 Data source and access
The source data are an IPUMS USA extract (a fixed-width *.dat.gz plus its *.xml DDI codebook) covering the 1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, and 1990 U.S. Census samples. The variables used are YEAR, SAMPLE, PERWT, SEX, AGE, BIRTHYR, MARST, GQ, and CHBORN. The sample-line weight SLWT, the allocation flag QCHBORN, and the nativity/birthplace variables NATIVITY/BPL were not requested; their absence is discussed in the limitations.
Formal citation:
Steven Ruggles, Sarah Flood, Matthew Sobek, Daniel Backman, Grace Cooper, Julia A. Rivera Drew, Stephanie Richards, Renae Rodgers, Jonathan Schroeder, and Kari C.W. Williams. IPUMS USA: Version 16.0 [dataset]. Minneapolis, MN: IPUMS, 2025. https://doi.org/10.18128/D010.V16.0
To reproduce the raw-data step, create your own IPUMS USA extract with the same samples and variables, place the .dat.gz and .xml files in data/raw/, and render this notebook locally. Only the aggregate CSVs in data/processed/ should be committed.

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
text:
2 Data processing
The code below locates the extract, reads it through its DDI codebook, and builds the aggregates presented in the following sections. It runs only when the raw extract is present and the cached outputs are out of date; otherwise the committed CSVs are loaded directly.


Code
# Packages for IPUMS parsing, data manipulation, CSV I/O, tables, and figures.
library(ipumsr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(readr)
library(knitr)
library(ggplot2)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

# Resolve paths whether rendered from the project root or the analysis directory.
project_root <- if (dir.exists("data")) "." else ".."
raw_dir <- file.path(project_root, "data", "raw")
processed_dir <- file.path(project_root, "data", "processed")
dir.create(processed_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

# Locate the extract by extension (not a hardcoded name), one data/DDI pair only.
one_file <- function(pattern, label) {
  files <- Sys.glob(file.path(raw_dir, pattern))
  if (length(files) != 1) {
    stop(
      "Expected exactly one ", label, " in ", raw_dir, "; found ",
      length(files), ".",
      call. = FALSE
    )
  }
  files
}

dat_gz_file <- one_file("*.dat.gz", "gzipped IPUMS .dat file")
ddi_file <- one_file("*.xml", "IPUMS DDI/XML codebook")

# The DDI supplies the fixed-width layout and the value labels used below.
ddi <- read_ipums_ddi(ddi_file)

# Variables parsed from the restricted person-level data.
analysis_vars <- c("YEAR", "PERWT", "SEX", "AGE", "BIRTHYR", "MARST", "GQ", "CHBORN")
ddi_display_vars <- analysis_vars

ddi_vars <- ipums_var_info(ddi)
missing_vars <- setdiff(ddi_display_vars, ddi_vars$var_name)
if (length(missing_vars) > 0) {
  stop(
    "The DDI is missing required variables: ",
    paste(missing_vars, collapse = ", "),
    call. = FALSE
  )
}

# Check that ipumsr can parse a few records using the DDI layout.
parse_check <- read_ipums_micro(
  ddi,
  vars = analysis_vars,
  n_max = 5,
  data_file = dat_gz_file,
  verbose = FALSE
)
stopifnot(nrow(parse_check) == 5)
rm(parse_check)

processing_version <- "common_universe_v5"

# Every aggregate file this notebook writes or expects to load.
output_inventory <- bind_rows(
  tibble(
    file = c(
      "ipums_preprocess_metadata.csv",
      "ipums_cohort_sample_sizes.csv",
      "ipums_fertility_counts_by_cohort.csv",
      "ipums_fertility_pmf_by_cohort.csv",
      "ipums_fertility_counts_by_cohort_weighted.csv",
      "ipums_fertility_pmf_by_cohort_weighted.csv",
      "validation_1950_direct_vs_proxy.csv",
      "validation_1950_direct_vs_proxy_pmf.csv",
      "ipums_age_cohort_validation.csv",
      "ipums_chborn_universe_by_cohort.csv",
      "ipums_cohort_fertility_checks.csv",
      "ipums_cohort_universe_summary.csv",
      "ipums_cohort_universe_pmf.csv",
      "ipums_universe_contrast_by_cohort.csv",
      "ipums_early_cohort_followup.csv"
    ),
    contents = c(
      "Processing version and rebuild timestamp.",
      "Main-series sample sizes by output cohort.",
      "Unweighted main-series fertility counts by cohort and child-count bucket.",
      "Unweighted main-series fertility PMF by cohort and child-count bucket.",
      "PERWT-weighted main-series fertility counts.",
      "PERWT-weighted main-series fertility PMF.",
      "Aggregate comparison of proxy 1950 and direct 1950 validation records.",
      "Child-count PMFs for proxy 1950 and direct 1950 validation records.",
      "Age range and derived birth-year span for each age-defined cohort source.",
      "Valid versus not-in-universe CHBORN counts by cohort source.",
      "Aggregate mean children and 12+ share by cohort source.",
      "Common-universe analysis: per decade-cohort x census x universe summary.",
      "Common-universe analysis: child-count PMFs per decade-cohort x census x universe.",
      "All-women vs ever-married contrast (mean shift, share-0 shift, TVD) by cohort.",
      "Early-cohort decomposition of universe vs mortality/decade effects."
    ),
    role = c(
      "Reproducibility check",
      "Downstream model input",
      "Downstream model input",
      "Downstream model input",
      "Sensitivity output",
      "Sensitivity output",
      "Validation output",
      "Validation output",
      "QA output",
      "QA output",
      "QA output",
      "Common-universe analysis",
      "Common-universe analysis",
      "Common-universe analysis",
      "Common-universe analysis"
    )
  ),
  tibble(
    file = paste0("fertility_pmf_", c(1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990), ".csv"),
    contents = "One unweighted main-series fertility PMF for the named output year.",
    role = "Downstream simulator input"
  )
)

processed_files <- output_inventory$file
processed_paths <- file.path(processed_dir, processed_files)
source_mtime <- max(file.info(c(dat_gz_file, ddi_file))$mtime)
force_rebuild <- tolower(Sys.getenv("KINCOUNTS_REBUILD_IPUMS", "false")) %in%
  c("true", "1", "yes")

# Rebuild if requested, if outputs are missing/stale, or if the processing
# version no longer matches the saved metadata.
metadata_path <- file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_preprocess_metadata.csv")
metadata_current <- file.exists(metadata_path) &&
  identical(
    read_csv(metadata_path, show_col_types = FALSE)$processing_version[[1]],
    processing_version
  )
processed_current <- all(file.exists(processed_paths)) &&
  min(file.info(processed_paths)$mtime) >= source_mtime &&
  metadata_current
rebuild_processed <- force_rebuild || !processed_current

# Local filenames and cache state (rendered in the appendix).
file_summary <- tibble(
  item = c(
    "compressed_data_file",
    "ddi_codebook",
    "compressed_size_mib",
    "processed_outputs_current",
    "rebuild_from_raw"
  ),
  value = c(
    basename(dat_gz_file),
    basename(ddi_file),
    round(file.info(dat_gz_file)$size / 1024^2, 1),
    processed_current,
    rebuild_processed
  )
)

# DDI variable positions (rendered in the appendix).
ddi_variables <- ddi_vars %>%
  filter(var_name %in% ddi_display_vars) %>%
  arrange(match(var_name, ddi_display_vars)) %>%
  select(var_name, var_label, start, end, imp_decim)

# DDI value labels that drive the coding rules (rendered in the coding section).
value_labels <- bind_rows(lapply(c("SEX", "CHBORN", "MARST"), function(var) {
  labels <- ddi_vars$val_labels[[match(var, ddi_vars$var_name)]]
  mutate(labels, var_name = var, .before = 1)
}))




Code
if (rebuild_processed) {
  # Parse only the variables used for filtering, cohort assignment, weights,
  # and fertility counts.
  ipums_data <- read_ipums_micro(
    ddi,
    vars = analysis_vars,
    data_file = dat_gz_file,
    verbose = FALSE
  ) %>%
    mutate(across(all_of(analysis_vars), as.numeric))

  # --- Main series: age-first cohort assignment (membership defined by AGE within
  # a census). The 1950 cohort is a proxy from women aged 60-69 in 1960; direct
  # 1950 (women 50-59 in 1950) is held out for validation. Birth-year spans are
  # derived afterwards for labeling only.
  cohort_records <- ipums_data %>%
    filter(SEX == 2) %>%
    mutate(
      source_year = YEAR,
      cohort_source = case_when(
        YEAR == 1950 & between(AGE, 50, 59) ~ "direct_1950_validation",
        YEAR == 1960 & between(AGE, 60, 69) ~ "proxy_1950",
        YEAR == 1960 & between(AGE, 50, 59) ~ "main_1960",
        YEAR == 1970 & between(AGE, 50, 59) ~ "main_1970",
        YEAR == 1980 & between(AGE, 50, 59) ~ "main_1980",
        YEAR == 1990 & between(AGE, 50, 59) ~ "main_1990",
        TRUE ~ NA_character_
      ),
      Year = case_when(
        cohort_source %in% c("proxy_1950", "direct_1950_validation") ~ 1950,
        cohort_source == "main_1960" ~ 1960,
        cohort_source == "main_1970" ~ 1970,
        cohort_source == "main_1980" ~ 1980,
        cohort_source == "main_1990" ~ 1990,
        TRUE ~ NA_real_
      ),
      series = case_when(
        cohort_source == "direct_1950_validation" ~ "validation",
        !is.na(cohort_source) ~ "main",
        TRUE ~ NA_character_
      ),
      valid_chborn = CHBORN > 0 & CHBORN < 97,
      n_child = if_else(valid_chborn, pmin(CHBORN - 1, 12), NA_real_)
    ) %>%
    filter(!is.na(cohort_source))

  cohort_composition <- cohort_records %>%
    group_by(series, cohort_source, source_year, Year) %>%
    summarise(
      n_records = n(),
      age_min = min(AGE, na.rm = TRUE),
      age_max = max(AGE, na.rm = TRUE),
      birthyr_min = min(BIRTHYR[BIRTHYR > 1800], na.rm = TRUE),
      birthyr_max = max(BIRTHYR[BIRTHYR > 1800], na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    mutate(cohort = paste0(birthyr_min, "-", birthyr_max)) %>%
    arrange(Year, source_year, cohort_source)

  cohort_records <- cohort_records %>%
    left_join(select(cohort_composition, cohort_source, cohort), by = "cohort_source")

  age_cohort_validation <- cohort_composition
  completed_fertility <- cohort_records
  valid_completed_fertility <- completed_fertility %>% filter(valid_chborn)
  main_fertility <- valid_completed_fertility %>% filter(series == "main")
  validation_1950 <- valid_completed_fertility %>%
    filter(cohort_source %in% c("proxy_1950", "direct_1950_validation"))

  # How much data the CHBORN validity rule excludes, by cohort source.
  chborn_universe <- completed_fertility %>%
    count(cohort_source, source_year, Year, cohort, valid_chborn, name = "n_records") %>%
    pivot_wider(
      names_from = valid_chborn,
      values_from = n_records,
      names_prefix = "valid_chborn_",
      values_fill = 0
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      total_records = valid_chborn_FALSE + valid_chborn_TRUE,
      valid_share = valid_chborn_TRUE / total_records
    ) %>%
    arrange(Year, source_year, cohort_source)

  cohort_means <- valid_completed_fertility %>%
    group_by(cohort_source, series, source_year, Year, cohort) %>%
    summarise(
      n_women = n(),
      n_women_weighted = sum(PERWT, na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_children = mean(n_child),
      mean_children_weighted = weighted.mean(n_child, PERWT, na.rm = TRUE),
      share_12plus = mean(n_child == 12),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    arrange(series, Year, source_year, cohort_source)

  fertility_counts <- main_fertility %>%
    count(Year, cohort, n_child, name = "n_women") %>%
    arrange(Year, n_child)

  fertility_counts_weighted <- main_fertility %>%
    group_by(Year, cohort, n_child) %>%
    summarise(n_women_weighted = sum(PERWT, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    arrange(Year, n_child)

  fertility_pmf <- fertility_counts %>%
    group_by(Year, cohort) %>%
    mutate(probability = n_women / sum(n_women)) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    transmute(Year, cohort, children = n_child, probability)

  fertility_pmf_weighted <- fertility_counts_weighted %>%
    group_by(Year, cohort) %>%
    mutate(probability_weighted = n_women_weighted / sum(n_women_weighted)) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    transmute(Year, cohort, children = n_child, probability_weighted)

  cohort_sample_sizes <- main_fertility %>%
    group_by(Year, cohort) %>%
    summarise(
      n_women = n(),
      n_women_weighted = sum(PERWT, na.rm = TRUE),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    arrange(Year)

  validation_1950_summary <- validation_1950 %>%
    group_by(cohort_source) %>%
    summarise(
      source_year = first(source_year),
      output_year = first(Year),
      n_women = n(),
      n_women_weighted = sum(PERWT, na.rm = TRUE),
      mean_children = mean(n_child),
      mean_children_weighted = weighted.mean(n_child, PERWT, na.rm = TRUE),
      share_12plus = mean(n_child == 12),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    arrange(cohort_source)

  validation_1950_pmf <- validation_1950 %>%
    count(cohort_source, n_child, name = "n_women") %>%
    left_join(
      validation_1950 %>%
        group_by(cohort_source, n_child) %>%
        summarise(n_women_weighted = sum(PERWT, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop"),
      by = c("cohort_source", "n_child")
    ) %>%
    group_by(cohort_source) %>%
    mutate(
      probability = n_women / sum(n_women),
      probability_weighted = n_women_weighted / sum(n_women_weighted)
    ) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    arrange(cohort_source, n_child)

  # --- Common-universe analysis (separate frame; the main series is untouched).
  # Follow decade birth cohorts across the 1960-1990 censuses and, where the whole
  # cohort has reached age 50+, contrast the children-ever-born distribution under
  # two universes: all women vs ever-married. This isolates the effect of the
  # universe shift (1950/1960 asked only ever-married women; 1970-1990 all women).
  # MARST: 1-5 = ever-married, 6 = never-married, 9 = blank/missing (kept in the
  # all-women universe but excluded from the ever-married subset).
  band_records <- ipums_data %>%
    filter(SEX == 2, between(YEAR, 1960, 1990), between(BIRTHYR, 1890, 1939)) %>%
    mutate(
      band_start = 10L * (BIRTHYR %/% 10L),
      birth_band = paste0(band_start, "-", band_start + 9L),
      universe_year = YEAR,
      # Keep only cells where the whole decade cohort has reached age 50+.
      cohort_complete = (universe_year - (band_start + 9L)) >= 50L,
      valid_chborn = CHBORN > 0 & CHBORN < 97,
      n_child = if_else(valid_chborn, pmin(CHBORN - 1, 12), NA_real_),
      marital = case_when(
        MARST %in% 1:5 ~ "ever_married",
        MARST == 6 ~ "never_married",
        TRUE ~ "unknown"
      ),
      gq_household = GQ %in% c(1, 2, 5)
    ) %>%
    filter(cohort_complete, valid_chborn)

  band_long <- bind_rows(
    band_records %>% mutate(universe = "all_women"),
    band_records %>% filter(marital == "ever_married") %>% mutate(universe = "ever_married")
  )

  band_summary <- band_long %>%
    group_by(birth_band, universe_year, universe) %>%
    summarise(
      age_min = min(AGE),
      age_max = max(AGE),
      n_women = n(),
      n_women_weighted = sum(PERWT),
      mean_children = mean(n_child),
      mean_children_weighted = weighted.mean(n_child, PERWT),
      var_children = var(n_child),
      share_0 = mean(n_child == 0),
      share_12plus = mean(n_child == 12),
      gq_household_share = mean(gq_household),
      .groups = "drop"
    ) %>%
    arrange(birth_band, universe_year, universe)

  band_pmf <- band_long %>%
    count(birth_band, universe_year, universe, n_child, name = "n_women") %>%
    left_join(
      band_long %>%
        group_by(birth_band, universe_year, universe, n_child) %>%
        summarise(n_women_weighted = sum(PERWT), .groups = "drop"),
      by = c("birth_band", "universe_year", "universe", "n_child")
    ) %>%
    group_by(birth_band, universe_year, universe) %>%
    mutate(
      probability = n_women / sum(n_women),
      probability_weighted = n_women_weighted / sum(n_women_weighted)
    ) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    arrange(birth_band, universe_year, universe, n_child)

  # Within-census contrast. The two universes coincide by construction before 1970
  # (never-married women were not in the CHBORN universe), so the contrast is
  # meaningful only for universe_year >= 1970.
  universe_contrast <- band_summary %>%
    select(birth_band, universe_year, universe, n_women, mean_children, var_children, share_0) %>%
    pivot_wider(
      names_from = universe,
      values_from = c(n_women, mean_children, var_children, share_0)
    ) %>%
    mutate(
      mean_shift = mean_children_all_women - mean_children_ever_married,
      share0_shift = share_0_all_women - share_0_ever_married,
      pct_never_married = 1 - n_women_ever_married / n_women_all_women
    )

  tvd_table <- band_pmf %>%
    select(birth_band, universe_year, universe, n_child, probability, probability_weighted) %>%
    pivot_wider(
      names_from = universe,
      values_from = c(probability, probability_weighted),
      values_fill = 0
    ) %>%
    group_by(birth_band, universe_year) %>%
    summarise(
      tvd = 0.5 * sum(abs(probability_all_women - probability_ever_married)),
      tvd_weighted = 0.5 * sum(abs(probability_weighted_all_women - probability_weighted_ever_married)),
      .groups = "drop"
    )

  universe_contrast <- universe_contrast %>%
    left_join(tvd_table, by = c("birth_band", "universe_year")) %>%
    arrange(birth_band, universe_year)

  # Early-cohort decomposition: the two cohorts seen ever-married at completed
  # fertility (1960) and all-women later (1970). Split the gap into the universe
  # effect (same census, adding never-married women) and the mortality/decade
  # effect (same ever-married universe, older age).
  pick_mean <- function(b, y, u) {
    v <- band_summary$mean_children[
      band_summary$birth_band == b &
        band_summary$universe_year == y &
        band_summary$universe == u
    ]
    if (length(v) == 1) v else NA_real_
  }
  early_cohort_followup <- tibble(
    birth_band = c("1890-1899", "1900-1909"),
    completed_year = c(1960, 1960),
    later_year = c(1970, 1970)
  ) %>%
    mutate(
      completed_em_mean = mapply(function(b, y) pick_mean(b, y, "ever_married"), birth_band, completed_year),
      later_em_mean = mapply(function(b, y) pick_mean(b, y, "ever_married"), birth_band, later_year),
      later_all_mean = mapply(function(b, y) pick_mean(b, y, "all_women"), birth_band, later_year),
      universe_effect = later_all_mean - later_em_mean,
      mortality_effect = later_em_mean - completed_em_mean
    )

  # Self-check: the 1900-1909 / 1960 ever-married mean should be close to the main
  # 1960 series mean, and 1890-1899 / 1960 close to the proxy-1950 mean.
  message(
    "Self-check (common-universe vs main series): ",
    "1900-1909/1960 ever-married mean = ", round(pick_mean("1900-1909", 1960, "ever_married"), 3),
    " (main_1960 ~ 2.429); ",
    "1890-1899/1960 ever-married mean = ", round(pick_mean("1890-1899", 1960, "ever_married"), 3),
    " (proxy_1950 ~ 2.818)."
  )

  # Write the aggregate CSVs.
  write_csv(cohort_sample_sizes, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_cohort_sample_sizes.csv"))
  write_csv(fertility_counts, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_fertility_counts_by_cohort.csv"))
  write_csv(fertility_pmf, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_fertility_pmf_by_cohort.csv"))
  write_csv(fertility_counts_weighted, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_fertility_counts_by_cohort_weighted.csv"))
  write_csv(fertility_pmf_weighted, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_fertility_pmf_by_cohort_weighted.csv"))
  write_csv(validation_1950_summary, file.path(processed_dir, "validation_1950_direct_vs_proxy.csv"))
  write_csv(validation_1950_pmf, file.path(processed_dir, "validation_1950_direct_vs_proxy_pmf.csv"))
  write_csv(age_cohort_validation, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_age_cohort_validation.csv"))
  write_csv(chborn_universe, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_chborn_universe_by_cohort.csv"))
  write_csv(cohort_means, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_cohort_fertility_checks.csv"))
  write_csv(band_summary, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_cohort_universe_summary.csv"))
  write_csv(band_pmf, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_cohort_universe_pmf.csv"))
  write_csv(universe_contrast, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_universe_contrast_by_cohort.csv"))
  write_csv(early_cohort_followup, file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_early_cohort_followup.csv"))
  write_csv(
    tibble(
      processing_version = processing_version,
      cohort_definition = "main series: age-defined cohorts (50-59; proxy-1950 from 60-69 in 1960; direct-1950 validation-only); CHBORN harmonized to 12+. Common-universe analysis: decade birth cohorts followed across 1960-1990 at completed ages, all-women vs ever-married (MARST 1-5).",
      rebuilt_at = as.character(Sys.time())
    ),
    file.path(processed_dir, "ipums_preprocess_metadata.csv")
  )

  fertility_pmf %>%
    group_split(Year) %>%
    lapply(function(dat) {
      year <- unique(dat$Year)
      write_csv(
        dat %>% transmute(children, probability),
        file.path(processed_dir, paste0("fertility_pmf_", year, ".csv"))
      )
    })

  rm(
    ipums_data, cohort_records, cohort_composition, completed_fertility,
    valid_completed_fertility, main_fertility, validation_1950,
    band_records, band_long, tvd_table
  )
} else {
  # Load the committed aggregates without reparsing restricted person-level data.
  rd <- function(f) read_csv(file.path(processed_dir, f), show_col_types = FALSE)
  cohort_sample_sizes <- rd("ipums_cohort_sample_sizes.csv")
  fertility_counts <- rd("ipums_fertility_counts_by_cohort.csv")
  fertility_pmf <- rd("ipums_fertility_pmf_by_cohort.csv")
  fertility_counts_weighted <- rd("ipums_fertility_counts_by_cohort_weighted.csv")
  fertility_pmf_weighted <- rd("ipums_fertility_pmf_by_cohort_weighted.csv")
  validation_1950_summary <- rd("validation_1950_direct_vs_proxy.csv")
  validation_1950_pmf <- rd("validation_1950_direct_vs_proxy_pmf.csv")
  age_cohort_validation <- rd("ipums_age_cohort_validation.csv")
  chborn_universe <- rd("ipums_chborn_universe_by_cohort.csv")
  cohort_means <- rd("ipums_cohort_fertility_checks.csv")
  band_summary <- rd("ipums_cohort_universe_summary.csv")
  band_pmf <- rd("ipums_cohort_universe_pmf.csv")
  universe_contrast <- rd("ipums_universe_contrast_by_cohort.csv")
  early_cohort_followup <- rd("ipums_early_cohort_followup.csv")
  preprocess_metadata <- rd("ipums_preprocess_metadata.csv")
  if (!identical(preprocess_metadata$processing_version[[1]], processing_version)) {
    stop(
      "Processed files were built with processing version ",
      preprocess_metadata$processing_version[[1]],
      "; expected ", processing_version,
      ". Re-render with KINCOUNTS_REBUILD_IPUMS=true.",
      call. = FALSE
    )
  }
}

# Confirm the complete aggregate output set is available after either branch.
written_files <- tibble(
  file = processed_files,
  status = if_else(file.exists(processed_paths), "available", "missing")
)
if (any(written_files$status == "missing")) {
  stop(
    "Missing processed files: ",
    paste(written_files$file[written_files$status == "missing"], collapse = ", "),
    call. = FALSE
  )
}

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
section: 3 The children-ever-born question and its universe
text:
3 The children-ever-born question and its universe
CHBORN records the number of children ever born alive to a woman. It counts live births only — stillbirths are excluded, as are adopted children and stepchildren.
For a multi-decade comparison, the central complication is that who was asked the question — the variable’s universe — changed across censuses:



Census
Universe (who was asked)




1950
ever-married women, and only the 1-in-6 sample line


1960
ever-married women


1970
all women age 15+


1980
all women age 15+


1990
all women age 15+



A woman not in the universe was not asked the question and has no recorded value (IPUMS code 00), which is distinct from a recorded count of zero. Such records are excluded from all fertility distributions. Two consequences follow.
Consequence 1 — coverage (1950). Because 1950 asked only ever-married sample-line women, most 1950 women have no CHBORN value. The figure below shows, for each census/age group, the share of women who were asked the question: direct-1950 is near 30% (only ever-married sample-line women); the 1960 census — the source of both the 1960 cohort and the 1950 proxy — is near 92% (ever-married women only); and the 1970–1990 censuses are at 100% (all women). This is why the 1950 cohort is taken from a proxy (the same birth cohort observed in 1960) rather than directly (see Defining the analytic sample).


Code
coverage_labels <- tibble(
  cohort_source = c("direct_1950_validation", "proxy_1950", "main_1960",
                    "main_1970", "main_1980", "main_1990"),
  slice = c("1950 cohort (direct)", "1950 cohort (proxy)", "1960 cohort",
            "1970 cohort", "1980 cohort", "1990 cohort"),
  who_asked = c("Ever-married, 1-in-6 sample line", "Ever-married women",
                "Ever-married women", "All women", "All women", "All women")
)

chborn_universe %>%
  left_join(coverage_labels, by = "cohort_source") %>%
  mutate(label = paste0(slice, ", ", source_year, " census")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = reorder(label, valid_share), y = valid_share, fill = who_asked)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::label_percent(accuracy = 1)(valid_share)),
            hjust = -0.15, size = 3.4) +
  coord_flip(clip = "off") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::label_percent(), limits = c(0, 1.1),
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0))) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Ever-married, 1-in-6 sample line" = "#D55E00",
    "Ever-married women" = "#E69F00",
    "All women" = "#0072B2"
  )) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Share of women in the age group who were asked CHBORN",
       fill = "Who the census asked",
       title = "Who was asked the children-ever-born question")








Figure 1: Of the women in each census/age group, the share who were asked the children-ever-born question (i.e., were in the variable’s universe). Colour indicates whom each census asked: in 1950 only ever-married women on the 1-in-6 sample line (~30%); in 1960 only ever-married women (~92%); from 1970 all women (~100%). The low 1950 coverage is why the 1950 cohort is taken from the 1960 proxy.





Consequence 2 — the universe shift (comparability). The early cohorts describe ever-married women only; the later cohorts describe all women. A raw cross-cohort series therefore mixes two populations: the 1970–1990 samples include never-married (largely childless) women that the 1950 and 1960 samples cannot. The only universe common to every census is ever-married women, which can be reconstructed in any year from marital status (MARST); this motivates the common-universe analysis below.

Cohort membership for the main series is defined by age within a census — women aged 50–59 — not by birth year, so nobody in the age window is dropped because their birthday had not yet occurred by the April-1 census date. title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
text:
5 CHBORN coding and harmonization
ipumsr returns the raw IPUMS codes. Code 00 is not applicable (not in universe — not zero children); zero children is code 01, one child 02, and so on. Per the IPUMS user note, children ever born is the code minus one, after dropping 00. We keep codes 01–61, set n_child = CHBORN − 1, and cap at 12 (12+) so all cohorts share one top category: the 1960–1990 samples top-code at 12+ (code 13), while 1950 resolves higher (to 25+, code 26).


Code
value_labels %>%
  filter(var_name %in% c("SEX", "CHBORN", "MARST")) %>%
  kable(caption = "DDI value labels for the variables that drive the coding rules.")
tibble(

  ddi_meaning = c(
    "N/A (not in universe)",
    "No children",
    "1 to 11 children",
    "12 children; 12+ in 1960-1990",
    "13 to 60 children (1950 coding)",
    "Unknown, illegible, or missing"

    "Drop (not zero children)",
    "n_child = 0",
    "n_child = CHBORN - 1",
    "n_child = 12 (the 12+ bucket)",
    "n_child = 12 (the 12+ bucket)",

) %>%

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
section: 6 Putting cohorts on a common universe
text:
6 Putting cohorts on a common universe
We treat fertility as effectively complete by age 50; the 50–59 age window is chosen to capture completed fertility while limiting the mortality selection that affects older ages. Under this assumption, differences in a fixed birth cohort’s measured children-ever-born distribution across successive censuses reflect measurement — mortality selection, migration, response error, and universe or top-code differences — rather than a change in fertility. We therefore estimate the universe effect by comparing the two universes within a single census, and use the cross-census comparison only to gauge the magnitude of these measurement differences.
We follow each decade birth cohort across the 1960–1990 censuses, restricting to cells where the whole cohort has reached age 50+, and within each census compute the children-ever-born distribution two ways: all women vs ever-married (MARST 1–5). Before 1970 the two coincide by construction (never-married women were not in the universe), so the contrast is informative only for 1970 onward.


Code
band_summary %>%
  mutate(Universe = recode(universe, all_women = "All women", ever_married = "Ever-married")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(universe_year, mean_children, color = Universe, group = Universe)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~ birth_band) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("All women" = "#D55E00", "Ever-married" = "#0072B2")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1960, 1970, 1980, 1990)) +
  labs(x = "Census year", y = "Mean children ever born",
       title = "Same birth cohorts under all-women vs ever-married universes")








Figure 2: Mean children ever born for each decade birth cohort, followed across censuses, under the two universes. The series coincide in 1960 (only ever-married women were asked) and separate from 1970 onward (all women asked); the gap between the series is the universe effect, while the drift along each line reflects mortality selection and decade differences from observing a cohort at older ages.







Code
band_pmf %>%
  filter(birth_band %in% c("1890-1899", "1900-1909"), universe_year == 1970) %>%
  mutate(Universe = recode(universe, all_women = "All women", ever_married = "Ever-married")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(n_child, probability, fill = Universe)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.75) +
  facet_wrap(~ birth_band) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("All women" = "#D55E00", "Ever-married" = "#0072B2")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:12) +
  labs(x = "Children ever born (12 = 12+)", y = "Probability",
       title = "All-women vs ever-married PMFs, early cohorts, 1970")








Figure 3: Children-ever-born PMFs for the two early cohorts in the first census where all women were asked (1970). Ever-married (the only universe available for these cohorts at completed fertility in 1960) vs all women. Including never-married women adds mass at zero and lowers the mean.







Code
universe_contrast %>%
  filter(universe_year >= 1970) %>%
  ggplot(aes(factor(universe_year), mean_shift, fill = birth_band)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.75) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linewidth = 0.3) +
  labs(x = "Census year", y = "Mean shift (all women − ever-married)",
       fill = "Birth cohort",
       title = "Effect of widening the universe to all women")








Figure 4: The universe effect: how much the mean falls when the universe is widened from ever-married to all women, by cohort and census (1970+ only, where the two universes differ).





The contrast and decomposition tables accompany the figures:


Code
universe_contrast %>%
  filter(universe_year >= 1970) %>%
  transmute(
    birth_band, universe_year,
    mean_all = mean_children_all_women,
    mean_em = mean_children_ever_married,
    mean_shift, share0_shift, pct_never_married, tvd
  ) %>%
  kable(digits = 3, caption = "All-women vs ever-married contrast by cohort and census (1970+). mean_shift = all − ever-married; pct_never_married = never-married share of the all-women cell; tvd = total-variation distance between the two PMFs.")
early_cohort_followup %>%
  kable(digits = 3, caption = "Early-cohort decomposition. universe_effect (all − ever-married in the later census) isolates the effect of adding never-married women; mortality_effect (ever-married, later − completion census) isolates survivor selection and decade artifacts.")

Interpretation. The within-census all-women vs ever-married contrast (mean_shift, tvd, and the 1970 PMFs) is the preferred estimate: it compares the same survivors in the same census, with appropriate PERWT weighting. Differences across censuses — the drift along each trajectory — bound the combined measurement difference rather than a change in fertility. Key caveats:

Mortality selection. Never-married women have higher old-age mortality, so the all-women pool drifts toward ever-married at older ages — the universe gap is attenuated at older ages. The least-contaminated estimate is the contrast at the youngest census in which a cohort is observed under the all-women universe.
Never-married fertility is a lower bound. Historically nonmarital births are under-reported, so the all-women mean understates true all-women fertility and the gap is, if anything, overstated.
Weighting. PERWT is appropriate for 1970–1990 (CHBORN is universal there), so the within-census contrast is unaffected by the weighting issue below. 1950 is excluded from this analysis (its sample-line weight SLWT is absent), and the 1960 cells are ever-married by construction.
Migration / nativity. Birth cohorts are not closed across censuses; NATIVITY/BPL are absent, so cross-census comparisons cannot be restricted to the native-born — a further reason to rely on the within-census contrast, which compares a single population. gq_household_share is reported per cell as a coarse check on institutionalization at older ages.

In sum, the pre-1970 cohorts (1950 and 1960) are observed only for ever-married women, which raises their measured completed fertility by roughly 0.1–0.2 children relative to an all-women basis. This gap is small next to the Depression-era decline in completed fertility across the 1901–1920 birth cohorts, and it is reported as a sensitivity rather than applied to the main series.

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
text:
7 Aggregate fertility distributions
The main-series outputs — completed-fertility distributions for women 50–59, with the 1950 proxy — are the inputs read by the downstream model and simulator. Means are reported unweighted, the form those downstream steps require; PERWT-weighted versions are written alongside as a sensitivity.


Code
cohort_means %>%
  filter(series == "main") %>%
  ggplot(aes(Year, mean_children)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8, color = "#0072B2") +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, color = "#0072B2") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(mean_children, 2)), vjust = -0.9, size = 3.3) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(1950, 1960, 1970, 1980, 1990)) +
  labs(x = "Cohort (census year)", y = "Mean children ever born",
       title = "Completed fertility by cohort")








Figure 5: Mean children ever born by main-series cohort (women 50-59; 1950 is the 1960 proxy). The dip for the 1901-1920 cohorts and the rise for the baby-boom mothers are demographically expected.







Code
fertility_pmf %>%
  ggplot(aes(children, probability)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0072B2", width = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ Year) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:12) +
  labs(x = "Children ever born (12 = 12+)", y = "Probability",
       title = "Fertility PMFs by cohort")








Figure 6: Fertility distributions (children ever born, 12 = 12+) for each main-series cohort.







Code
cohort_sample_sizes %>%
  kable(digits = 0, caption = "Main-series cohort sample sizes (unweighted and PERWT-weighted).")
cohort_means %>%
  filter(series == "main") %>%
  transmute(Year, cohort, n_women, mean_children, mean_children_weighted, share_12plus) %>%

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
text:
8 Validation: direct vs proxy 1950
The 1950 cohort is represented by the 1960 proxy. As a check, we compare it against the held-out direct-1950 records (women 50–59 in the 1950 census). The unweighted distributions are nearly identical, which corroborates the proxy. (The weighted direct-1950 number is unreliable — 1950 CHBORN is a sample-line variable and the correct weight SLWT is not in the extract — so we rely on the unweighted comparison, and tail differences above 12 are affected by the top-code asymmetry noted in the coding section.)


Code
validation_1950_pmf %>%
  mutate(Source = recode(cohort_source,
                         direct_1950_validation = "Direct 1950",
                         proxy_1950 = "Proxy (1960)")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(n_child, probability, fill = Source)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(width = 0.8), width = 0.75) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Direct 1950" = "#D55E00", "Proxy (1960)" = "#0072B2")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 0:12) +
  labs(x = "Children ever born (12 = 12+)", y = "Probability",
       title = "1950 cohort: direct vs proxy")








Figure 7: Children-ever-born PMFs (unweighted): direct 1950 (women 50-59 in 1950) vs the 1960 proxy (women 60-69 in 1960). Near-identical shapes corroborate using the proxy.

  kable(digits = 3, caption = "Direct 1950 vs proxy 1950 summary. Unweighted means agree closely; the weighted direct-1950 number is approximate (no SLWT).")

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
text:
9 Limitations

12+ top-code. All outputs harmonize the child-count support to 0, 1, …, 11, 12+. 1960–1990 already top-code at 12+, but 1950 resolves to 25+, so the cap discards tail detail for the (validation-only) 1950 series and for older cohorts seen in 1950.
Weighting. Main outputs are unweighted; PERWT-weighted versions are provided as a sensitivity. The 1950 sample-line weight SLWT is absent, so any 1950 weighted figure is approximate; 1950 is therefore kept out of the weighted cross-cohort series.
Extract gaps. A future extract should add SLWT (correct 1950 weighting), NATIVITY/BPL (restrict to native-born / measure cohort compositional drift across censuses), and QCHBORN (filter allocated/imputed values). Each would close a caveat that the current data cannot resolve.
Sharing. The restricted IPUMS records, person-level weights, and raw fixed-width data remain local-only and are not committed; only the aggregate CSVs are.

title: Preprocessing IPUMS fertility data
text:
10 Appendix: reproducibility and outputs
This page is cache-aware: it rebuilds the aggregates from the raw extract only when the extract is present and the processing version or file timestamps indicate the cached outputs are out of date, and otherwise loads the committed CSVs. Set KINCOUNTS_REBUILD_IPUMS=true to force a rebuild.

title: Home
text:
Welcome to the kincounts research website.


This project models how fertility distributions shape sibling and broader kin-count distributions. It estimates fertility patterns from IPUMS data, derives sibling distributions, and simulates relative counts under alternative fertility models.




Fertility Distribution Estimation: Fit zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) models to fertility count data
Relative Count Simulation: Generate simulated counts of relatives at various degrees




We provide a Shiny web application for interactive exploration of the models. The app includes:

Parameter estimation from uploaded fertility data
Customizable simulations with adjustable parameters
Visualization of relative count distributions




The application uses a zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) model to capture fertility patterns, including childlessness. The analyses use those fertility models to derive sibling distributions and simulate counts of relatives across relationship categories.




analysis/: R Markdown analysis files
app/: Shiny application code
code/: Utility functions and analysis scripts
data/: Example datasets
docs/: Generated website files
output/: Analysis outputs




Explore the analyses using the navigation bar above, or run the Shiny application locally:
shiny::runApp("app/app.R")
